content.text: A change impact assessment (CIA) for GenAI quantifies how much a specific AI deployment changes people, processes, and systems in a particular part of the organization. Without this assessment, OCM efforts apply generic support to everyone equally — which means over-investing where change is minimal and under-investing where it is substantial. The CIA is the prioritization engine for the entire OCM plan.

attrs.alt: Change impact assessment framework showing four dimensions scored in a matrix by role or team

content.text: A change impact assessment is a structured analysis that examines, for each affected stakeholder group, how significantly their day-to-day work will change as a result of a GenAI deployment. It evaluates impact across multiple dimensions — typically people, process, systems, and culture — and produces a score that guides the intensity of OCM support required.

A CIA is not a risk assessment (which evaluates probability and severity of failure) nor a requirements document (which specifies system behavior). It is specifically focused on the human change burden: how much will people need to change how they work, and how prepared are they to make that change?

Change impact assessments prevent two common OCM failures. The first is under-investment: rolling out a GenAI tool to customer service agents without recognizing that it replaces 40% of their daily task pattern, and then providing only a one-hour onboarding session. The second is over-investment: running a full change program for a back-office analytics team that adopts a new AI dashboard as a minor addition to existing tooling.

The CIA also creates a factual basis for conversations with executives about OCM resource requirements. Rather than requesting support based on gut feel, the OCM lead can show: "These five role clusters have high impact scores; here is the evidence, and here is the support they need."
Enables prioritized allocation of OCM resources to the groups with the highest change burden
Surfaces hidden impact hotspots that executives may not have anticipated
Creates a shared factual baseline that aligns IT, HR, business owners, and OCM on the scope of change
Provides evidence-based justification for OCM investment level and timeline
Guides training curriculum design by identifying which roles need deep skill-building vs. light orientation
Identifies process redesign needs that must be addressed before training can be effective
Enables persona-based change management: different interventions for different impact profiles
A thorough CIA requires time before the deployment — which can feel like it delays progress
Scores are based on estimates and subject to stakeholder framing bias — high-influence groups may overstate impact to get more support
A CIA conducted without talking directly to affected employees produces low-quality scores based on management assumptions
As GenAI tools evolve post-deployment, the CIA becomes stale and needs refresh — an ongoing cost
The process may surface politically sensitive findings about which departments will be most disrupted

A CIA proceeds in four steps. First, identify all stakeholder groups affected by the GenAI deployment — organized by role, department, geography, or any other segmentation meaningful to the organization. Second, for each group, score impact on each dimension (people, process, systems, culture) on a 1-5 scale using a combination of structured interviews, process observations, and tool analysis. Third, aggregate the scores to a total impact tier (high, medium, low). Fourth, map each impact tier to a support intensity level in the OCM plan.

The scoring interviews should be conducted with frontline employees and their direct managers — not with department heads speaking on behalf of their teams. Department heads systematically underestimate the change burden on frontline workers.
The four impact dimensions and their sub-criteria:
Less than 10% of job tasks change
More than 50% of job tasks change
Skill exists in the team today
Skill requires 40+ hours of new learning
No change in who decides what
Human decisions delegated to or from AI
Requires ERP/CRM integration and data migration
New data types, new privacy scope, full audit required
Team already uses AI tools comfortably
Team has never used AI; skepticism is high
Failure is not tolerated; team will avoid AI if risky
Before finalizing the OCM plan structure: impact scores drive OCM resource allocation decisions
Before selecting the pilot cohort: choose a group with manageable impact score to validate the change approach
Before designing training curricula: impact profiles reveal which roles need deep vs. light learning tracks
When a new department or geography is added to the rollout scope mid-program
At 6-month intervals post-launch to identify where the CIA scores have shifted as AI usage has evolved
When stakeholders dispute the level of change management support being provided

Step 1 — Scope the assessment: List all roles and departments in scope. For each, estimate the number of employees affected. Prioritize the largest and most mission-critical groups for full CIA interviews.

Step 2 — Design the scoring instrument: Create a structured interview guide with 8-12 questions that map to the impact dimensions. Include both structured (1-5 scale) and open-text questions to capture nuance the scale misses.
Step 3 — Conduct stakeholder interviews: Interview 3-5 frontline employees and their manager per role cluster. Avoid group interviews; social dynamics suppress honest assessment.

Step 4 — Score and validate: Score each dimension based on interview responses. Share the scores with department leaders for a 48-hour validation window — they can challenge scores with evidence but not override without substantiation.
Step 5 — Produce the impact heat map: Visualize scores by role cluster and dimension. Use the heat map in OCM planning sessions to demonstrate where investment is most needed.

Step 6 — Map scores to OCM support packages: Define what High, Medium, and Low impact tiers receive. Example: High tier gets dedicated change agent, 3-module training track, 6-week pilot with bi-weekly check-ins. Medium tier gets 2-module track and monthly check-ins. Low tier gets self-serve onboarding and email communications only.

# Change Impact Assessment scoring engine
# Aggregates multi-dimension scores into impact tier and recommended support

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class ImpactScore:
    role: str
    department: str
    people: int        # 1-5
    process: int       # 1-5
    systems: int       # 1-5
    culture: int       # 1-5

    def total(self) -> int:
        return self.people + self.process + self.systems + self.culture

    def tier(self) -> Literal["HIGH", "MEDIUM", "LOW"]:
        t = self.total()
        if t >= 14:
            return "HIGH"
        elif t >= 8:
            return "MEDIUM"
        else:
            return "LOW"

    def support_package(self) -> str:
        packages = {
            "HIGH": "Dedicated change agent + 3-module training + weekly check-ins + 8-week pilot",
            "MEDIUM": "Shared change agent + 2-module training + bi-weekly check-ins",
            "LOW": "Self-serve onboarding + 1-module orientation + monthly newsletter",
        }
        return packages[self.tier()]

    def priority_dimension(self) -> str:
        scores = {
            "People": self.people,
            "Process": self.process,
            "Systems": self.systems,
            "Culture": self.culture,
        }
        return max(scores, key=scores.get)

# Example assessment data
assessments = [
    ImpactScore("Customer Service Agent", "Operations", people=5, process=4, systems=2, culture=4),
    ImpactScore("Financial Analyst", "Finance", people=3, process=5, systems=4, culture=3),
    ImpactScore("Software Engineer", "Engineering", people=2, process=3, systems=5, culture=2),
    ImpactScore("Marketing Copywriter", "Marketing", people=4, process=2, systems=2, culture=3),
    ImpactScore("Executive Assistant", "Corporate", people=3, process=3, systems=2, culture=2),
]

print(f"{'Role':<30} {'Dept':<15} {'Total':>6} {'Tier':>8} {'Priority Dim':>15}")
print("-" * 80)
for a in assessments:
    print(f"{a.role:<30} {a.department:<15} {a.total():>6} {a.tier():>8} {a.priority_dimension():>15}")

# Outputs:
# Customer Service Agent         Operations          15     HIGH          People
# Financial Analyst              Finance             15     HIGH         Process
# Software Engineer              Engineering         12   MEDIUM         Systems
# Marketing Copywriter           Marketing           11   MEDIUM          People
# Executive Assistant            Corporate           10   MEDIUM          People

## Change Impact Assessment — Interview Guide (Frontline Employee)

Time: 45-60 minutes | Format: Individual, confidential

### Section 1 — Current State (Baseline)
1. Walk me through a typical day. What tasks take the most time?
2. Which of those tasks involve reviewing, creating, or analyzing information?
3. What tools do you currently use for those tasks?
4. What would you say are your strongest professional skills in this role?

### Section 2 — AI Tool Awareness
5. What do you know about the AI tool being introduced? Where did you hear about it?
6. Have you used any AI tools in your personal or professional life? What was that like?

### Section 3 — Impact Assessment
7. Looking at what the tool does, which of your current tasks do you think it will change?
   [Probe: How much of your day does that represent?]
8. Are there tasks you currently do that you think the tool might handle entirely?
   [Probe: How do you feel about that?]
9. Do you think you have the skills today to use this tool effectively? What would you need to learn?
10. How do you think your team will react to this change?
11. What would need to be true for you to feel confident using AI in your work?

### Section 4 — Process and Systems
12. Are there approval steps, sign-offs, or handoffs in your workflow that might change?
13. Are there systems or tools you use that you'd expect AI to need to connect with?

### Scoring Notes (for OCM analyst — not shared with interviewee)
- Q7-8 -> People score (task change scope)
- Q9 -> People score (skill gap)
- Q10-11 -> Culture score
- Q12 -> Process score
- Q13 -> Systems score

A global insurance company deployed an AI underwriting assistant to 800 underwriters across 12 countries. Initial CIA scores for underwriters came in at 16/20 (HIGH) due to high process impact (AI would change the decision authority structure) and high culture impact (underwriters had strong professional identity tied to independent judgment). The CIA finding drove the decision to co-design the AI-assisted workflow with a 15-person underwriter working group rather than imposing a mandated process. The co-design took six weeks longer than the original deployment plan, but adoption reached 72% within 90 days — compared to a 31% adoption rate in a parallel country that bypassed the CIA and co-design step.
Conducting CIAs with only department heads and skipping frontline employee interviews — management assumptions are systematically inaccurate
Treating the CIA as a one-time event rather than refreshing it as the AI tool evolves and adoption deepens
Using the CIA to justify a predetermined OCM investment level rather than letting scores drive investment
Assigning identical impact scores to all roles in a department when role-level variation is the norm
Conflating impact score with resistance score — high-impact roles are not necessarily more resistant
Failing to include systems impact in the CIA, leaving IT integration timelines out of OCM planning
Interview frontline employees directly, not proxies — their experience of the change is what the CIA must capture
Validate scores with department leaders in a structured session, not email — enable real-time challenge and evidence
Publish the heat map to OCM steering committee to create shared understanding of where investment is most needed
Use CIA scores to drive training curriculum decisions, not just OCM support intensity
Separate people impact from process impact — they require different interventions even when both are high
Refresh the CIA at 6-month intervals: what was HIGH at launch may become LOW as skills develop
Include geography and language as impact dimensions for global rollouts — local context creates significant variation

Beyond role-level scoring, effective CIAs group stakeholders into impact personas that cut across departmental boundaries. A persona groups people who share a similar impact profile regardless of their department. This reduces repetitive analysis and enables standardized support packages for structurally similar roles.
Three common GenAI impact personas emerge across industries:

The Craft Worker persona: roles where professional identity is deeply tied to the skill being augmented. Lawyers, physicians, senior analysts, and designers often fall here. These roles have high culture impact scores and require autonomy-preserving workflow designs.

The Volume Processor persona: roles that handle high volumes of repetitive information processing (data entry, document review, call center triage). These roles typically have high people impact and high process impact scores, as AI replaces large fractions of current task patterns.

The Decision Maker persona: roles where AI changes the information available to decisions rather than the decision itself. Managers, executives, and clinical directors often fall here. Culture and process scores are typically high; systems scores may be low.

Mapping your affected population to these three personas helps the OCM team develop targeted interventions once rather than re-inventing them for each department. The persona profile also determines the primary resistance type to anticipate: Craft Workers resist autonomy loss, Volume Processors fear job displacement, and Decision Makers resist trust deficits in AI-generated information.

To build the persona map, cross-reference the CIA dimension scores with the role profiles from the stakeholder analysis. Roles with high People + Culture scores and moderate-to-low Systems scores are Craft Workers. Roles with high People + Process scores and moderate Systems scores are Volume Processors. Roles with high Process + Culture and low People scores are Decision Makers. The mapping is probabilistic — some roles will display mixed profiles, which indicates the need for a blended intervention approach.

For a deployment affecting 50 roles, a CIA can be completed by two analysts in 3-4 weeks with 45-minute interviews per role cluster sample. For 200+ roles, use a tiered approach: conduct full interviews for the 20 most critical roles, and use structured survey instruments for the remaining groups. Automated analytics from the existing HR system (tenure, role type, prior training completion rates) can supplement survey data to reduce interview burden while maintaining scoring quality.
Impact heat maps and stakeholder matrices
CIA documentation linked to ADKAR tracking
Large populations where interviews are infeasible
Rough-cut prioritization only, not for planning
Only appropriate for single-role, trivial tool additions
CIA interview notes and scores are HR-sensitive and should be stored in secured systems with access controls
Anonymize individual interview responses before sharing aggregate CIA findings with business leaders
CIA documents should be version-controlled; retain the original baseline CIA for post-launch comparison
Impact scores for specific business units should not be shared externally or with employees who may react adversarially
Where CIA findings reveal process risks (e.g., AI making decisions that require human sign-off by regulation), escalate to legal and compliance immediately
A change impact assessment quantifies how much each stakeholder group's work changes — it is the prioritization engine for the OCM plan
Four dimensions — people, process, systems, and culture — each require different interventions; scores must be dimension-level, not only aggregate
Interviews with frontline employees are mandatory; management proxy assessments systematically underestimate change burden
CIA scores drive OCM support intensity: high-impact groups need dedicated change agents, not just email communications
The CIA should be refreshed at 6-month intervals post-launch; what was high impact at deployment may be low after skill development
A well-conducted CIA creates a shared factual basis for OCM resource conversations with executive stakeholders
CIA findings may reveal process redesign or systems integration needs that must be resolved before training can be effective
